1: The LORD was gracious to Sarah as he had said, and the LORD did for Sarah what he had promised — the chapter opens with a double statement of fulfillment: “as he had said” and “what he had promised.” The divine promise-keeping that has seemed so long delayed is now enacted. Paul makes this the model for gospel faith: “No matter how many promises God has made, they are ‘Yes’ in Christ. And so through him the ‘Amen’ is spoken by us to the glory of God” (2 Corinthians 1:20). The yes of God to Sarah’s barren womb is the prototype of the ultimate yes that God speaks to all his promises in the person and work of Christ.
2: Sarah became pregnant and bore a son to Abraham in his old age, at the very time God had promised him — “at the very time God had promised” is the key phrase: the birth occurs on the divine timetable, not the human one. Paul uses this same temporal precision for Christ’s coming: “when the set time had fully come, God sent his Son, born of a woman, born under the law” (Galatians 4:4). The “set time” (plēroma tou chronou) of the incarnation corresponds to “the very time God had promised” for Isaac’s birth. Divine faithfulness operates on a timetable that human impatience cannot accelerate; the wait for the promise is itself part of the covenant pedagogy.
3: Abraham gave the name Isaac to the son Sarah bore him — Isaac means “he laughs” or “laughter” (see v6). The naming of the child of promise is an act of covenant recognition: this is the son through whom the promise runs. Paul reads Isaac as the type of believers who are “children of promise” (Galatians 4:28: “Now you, brothers and sisters, like Isaac, are children of promise”). The child of promise who is named and recognized is the type of the one who names the covenant children: “I will acknowledge their name before my Father and his angels” (Revelation 3:5). Christ names and acknowledges those who are his through the promised Spirit.
4: Abraham circumcised Isaac when his son was eight days old, as God had commanded him — the covenant sign applied to the child of promise on the eighth day. The eighth-day circumcision (Leviticus 12:3; Philippians 3:5: “circumcised on the eighth day”) marks membership in the covenant community. Christ is circumcised on the eighth day (Luke 2:21: “when the time came for them to be circumcised according to the law”), entering the covenant community he came to fulfill. Paul in Colossians 2:11–12 connects circumcision to baptism: “In him you were also circumcised with a circumcision not performed by human hands… having been buried with him in baptism.” The eighth-day covenant sign is fulfilled in Christ’s own submission to it and in the new-covenant circumcision of the heart.
5: Abraham was a hundred years old when his son Isaac was born — the chronological marker that emphasizes the impossibility of the birth by natural means. Paul uses this explicitly in Romans 4: “Without weakening in his faith, he faced the fact that his body was as good as dead — since he was about a hundred years old — and that Sarah’s womb was also dead. Yet he did not waver through unbelief” (Romans 4:19–20). The hundred-year-old father and the ninety-year-old mother are the impossibility that faith transcends. Christ’s resurrection is the ultimate impossibility that faith embraces: “God who gives life to the dead and calls into being things that were not” (Romans 4:17) — the same God who gave life to Sarah’s dead womb raises Christ from the dead.
6: Sarah said, “God has brought me laughter, and everyone who hears about this will laugh with me” — the laughter of fulfilled promise. Sarah laughed in disbelief when the promise was announced (Genesis 18:12); she laughs in joy when it is fulfilled. The transformation of incredulous laughter to joyful laughter is the pattern of the gospel’s reception: the news of the resurrection is met with disbelief (Matthew 28:17: “some doubted”), but those who receive it enter the joy of fulfilled promise: “Though you have not seen him, you love him; and even though you do not see him now, you believe in him and are filled with an inexpressible and glorious joy” (1 Peter 1:8). The laughter at Isaac’s birth prefigures the eschatological joy at the resurrection.
7: “Who would have said to Abraham that Sarah would nurse children? Yet I have borne him a son in his old age” — the wonderment at the impossible fulfilled. The “who would have said” is the OT pattern of amazement at divine action that exceeds human expectation: “Who has believed our message and to whom has the arm of the LORD been revealed?” (Isaiah 53:1). The gospel’s announcement of a crucified Messiah is similarly “a stumbling block to Jews and foolishness to Gentiles” (1 Corinthians 1:23) until it is received as the power and wisdom of God. Sarah’s amazement at the impossible birth is the prototype of the apostolic amazement at the resurrection.
8: The child grew and was weaned, and Abraham held a great feast on the day Isaac was weaned — the feast celebrating the weaned child’s growing independence is a type of the eschatological feast that Christ will host. Jesus himself uses feasting as the primary image for the kingdom: “the kingdom of heaven is like a king who prepared a wedding banquet for his son” (Matthew 22:2); “Many will come from east and west and recline at table with Abraham, Isaac and Jacob in the kingdom of heaven” (Matthew 8:11). The feast at Isaac’s weaning is the first of several covenant feasts in the patriarchal narratives; the eschatological feast with Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob is the fulfillment that Christ announces.
9: Sarah saw the son of Hagar the Egyptian mocking — the conflict between the two sons that Paul interprets as the conflict between the two covenants. Galatians 4:29: “at that time the son born according to the flesh persecuted the son born by the Spirit.” Paul applies this dynamic to his own situation: those who insist on circumcision and the law “persecute” those who live by faith in the promise. The mocking of the child of promise by the child of human arrangement is the historical type of the law’s burden on those who seek to live by faith. Christ frees believers from this burden: “It is for freedom that Christ has set us free. Stand firm, then, and do not let yourselves be burdened again by a yoke of slavery” (Galatians 5:1).
10: Sarah said, “Get rid of that slave woman and her son, for that woman's son will never share in the inheritance with my son Isaac” — Paul quotes this verse directly in Galatians 4:30: “Get rid of the slave woman and her son, for the slave woman's son will never share in the inheritance with the free woman's son.” Paul applies it to the incompatibility of law-righteousness and faith-righteousness as paths to the inheritance. The inheritance — justification before God, eternal life, the new creation — comes exclusively through the promise-line (through Christ) and not through the human-effort line (through the law). The two cannot coexist as parallel paths to the inheritance.
11: The matter distressed Abraham greatly because it concerned his son — the pain of separating from Ishmael. The divine commands that are painful to obey are the tests of covenant loyalty; the Akedah (Genesis 22) will push this further. Abraham’s grief over Ishmael anticipates the grief of the Father who “did not spare his own Son” (Romans 8:32). The paternal love that must nonetheless yield to the divine command is the typological structure of the Akedah. Here it is the sending away of Ishmael; in Genesis 22 it is the offering of Isaac; in the NT it is the giving of the Son. The sequence of paternal sacrifices deepens toward the cross.
12: God said to Abraham, “Do not be so distressed about the boy and your slave woman. Listen to whatever Sarah tells you, because it is through Isaac that your offspring will be reckoned” — the divine narrowing of the covenant promise to the Isaac line is confirmed. Paul quotes the end of this verse in Romans 9:7: “Not all who are descended from Israel are Israel… it is through Isaac that your offspring will be reckoned.” Paul uses it to establish that physical descent from Abraham does not constitute covenant membership; the covenant runs through the promise-line (Isaac) and ultimately through the one seed who is Christ (Galatians 3:16). The divine declaration “it is through Isaac” becomes “it is through Christ” in the NT’s reading of the covenant.
13: “I will make the son of the slave woman into a nation also, because he is your offspring” — God’s care for Ishmael and the non-covenant line. The divine promise to Ishmael (also Genesis 16:10–11) shows that those outside the specific covenant line are not outside God’s general care and providence. Paul’s anguish for Israel in Romans 9:1–5 reflects the same theological tension: those who are outside the current covenant-fulfillment (Israel not yet believing in Christ) are still loved and within God’s larger purposes (Romans 11:25–26: “all Israel will be saved”). Christ’s mission includes the “other sheep” (John 10:16: “I have other sheep that are not of this sheep pen”) — the Ishmael-line nations that are not the primary covenant line but are within the scope of God’s concern.
14: Early next morning Abraham took food and a skin of water and gave them to Hagar, setting them on her shoulders and sending her off with the boy — the sending away of Hagar and Ishmael mirrors the Exodus (Exodus 12:39: Israel set out in haste) and anticipates the exile. Those who are sent away by the covenant community are not beyond God’s reach: vv17–20 show God finding and providing for Hagar and Ishmael in the desert. Christ seeks the lost and the expelled: “the Son of Man came to seek and to save the lost” (Luke 19:10); “he saw a great crowd, and he had compassion on them, because they were like sheep without a shepherd” (Matthew 9:36). The God who sends and the God who seeks are not in contradiction.
15: When the water in the skin was gone, Hagar put the boy under one of the bushes — the extremity of thirst and near-death. The water-to-thirst-to-divine-provision pattern runs through the wilderness narratives (Exodus 15:22–25; Genesis 17:1–7) and reaches its fulfillment in Christ who offers living water that permanently quenches spiritual thirst: “Whoever drinks the water I give them will never thirst. Indeed, the water I give them will become in them a spring of water welling up to eternal life” (John 4:14). The water that runs out in the desert is the type of all earthly provision that eventually fails; Christ is the provision that does not run out.
16: Hagar sat at a distance, weeping, unable to watch the boy die — the grief of a mother who cannot save her dying child. The maternal grief at the death of a child reaches its depth in the NT at the cross: Mary stands watching her son die (John 19:25–27). Where Hagar cannot save Ishmael and weeps in helplessness, God hears the boy’s cry (v17). Where Mary cannot save Jesus, God raises him on the third day. In both cases, the human mother can only witness; divine intervention alone brings rescue and life. The weeping mother’s helplessness is the foil that reveals God as the sole source of life.
17: God heard the boy crying, and the angel of God called to Hagar from heaven: “What is the matter, Hagar? Do not be afraid; God has heard the boy crying” — the angel of God (here; the “angel of the LORD” in Genesis 16) calls from heaven to answer the cry of the dying. “Do not be afraid” is the recurring divine assurance at every moment of divine appearance. The God who hears the boy’s cry answers through the angelic messenger, demonstrating the divine attentiveness to those outside the primary covenant line. Christ’s healing ministry similarly extends to those outside the expected circle: the Syrophoenician woman’s daughter (Mark 7:24–30), the Samaritan leper (Luke 17:16), the centurion’s servant (Matthew 8:5–13). “Everyone who calls on the name of the Lord will be saved” (Romans 10:13) — including Ishmael’s line.
18: “Lift the boy up and take him by the hand, for I will make him into a great nation” — the divine lifting of the fallen and the promise of greatness through the one who was nearly lost. The “lifting up” of the dying boy connects to the resurrection pattern: “lifted up from the ground” (John 12:32, where Jesus speaks of being “lifted up” on the cross and drawing all people to himself). The promise “I will make him into a great nation” — even Ishmael, even the expelled, even the nearly dead — is the grace that extends beyond the narrow covenant channel, anticipating the gospel’s reach to all nations through Christ.
19: God opened her eyes and she saw a well of water — the divine opening of eyes to see what was already there. The well was present in the desert; Hagar simply could not see it. The pattern of eyes opened to see what was previously invisible is a recurring gospel motif: the disciples’ eyes opened to recognize the risen Christ (Luke 24:31: “Then their eyes were opened and they recognized him”); the blind receiving sight (John 9); Elisha’s servant’s eyes opened to see the heavenly army (2 Kings 6:17). Christ opens eyes — physically in his miracles, spiritually through the Spirit — to see the provision and the presence that were always there but invisible to the natural eye.
20: God was with the boy as he grew up. He lived in the desert and became an archer — the divine presence with Ishmael outside the covenant community is a type of God’s general providence with all people. Matthew 5:45: “He causes his sun to rise on the evil and the good, and sends rain on the righteous and the unrighteous.” God’s common grace accompanies Ishmael’s desert life; this is not saving grace (which comes through the covenant-promise line that culminates in Christ) but the sustaining providence that keeps all people alive and capable of the dignity that God’s image requires (Acts 17:28: “in him we live and move and have our being”).
21: While living in the Desert of Paran, his mother got a wife for him from Egypt — Ishmael’s settlement in Paran and marriage to an Egyptian connects him to the region between Sinai and Canaan, the territory through which Israel will later journey in the Exodus. The Egyptian wife continues the Hagar-Egyptian lineage. This is a canonical-context note: Ishmael’s descendants settle in the region that becomes the background for the Exodus, which itself becomes the type of Christ’s redemption. The geographic situating of Ishmael’s line is part of the broader mapping of peoples that the table of nations (Genesis 10) and subsequent genealogies provide.
22: Abimelech and Phicol said to Abraham, “God is with you in everything you do” — the Gentile recognition of the divine presence with the covenant-bearer. The pagan leaders see in Abraham what Israel’s neighbors will later see in Joseph (Genesis 39:3: “His master saw that the LORD was with him”), Daniel (Daniel 6:23), and ultimately in Christ himself: “No one could perform the signs you are doing if God were not with him” (John 3:2). The divine presence recognized by outsiders is the canonical testimony to the covenant-bearer’s legitimacy. Christ is Emmanuel, “God with us” (Matthew 1:23) — the one in whom divine presence is not an observable quality but the literal truth.
23: Abimelech asked Abraham to swear not to deal falsely with him or his descendants — a covenant of non-aggression between the covenant-bearer and the surrounding peoples. The covenant that extends to “my children and my descendants” is the multi-generational scope of covenant commitment. Christ establishes a new covenant that also reaches across generations: “The promise is for you and your children and for all who are far off — for all whom the Lord our God will call” (Acts 2:39). The covenant of mutual faithfulness between Abraham and Abimelech is the horizontal-covenant pattern of which the new covenant between God and humanity through Christ is the vertical fulfillment.
24: Abraham said, “I swear it” — the covenant oath. The covenant sealed by an oath is the pattern that Hebrews applies to both the Abrahamic covenant and Christ’s high priesthood: “Others became priests without any oath, but he became a priest with an oath when God said to him: ‘The Lord has sworn and will not change his mind: “You are a priest forever.”’” (Hebrews 7:20–21). The oath that ratifies the covenant is the divine guarantee of its permanence. Abraham’s “I swear it” to Abimelech is the human covenant-oath pattern; God’s oath to make Christ a priest forever is the divine covenant-oath that secures the new covenant beyond all contingency.
25: Abraham complained to Abimelech about a well of water that Abimelech's servants had seized — the well-dispute that leads to the Beer-sheba covenant. The well is a site of both conflict and resolution throughout the patriarchal narratives (Isaac’s wells in Genesis 26:19–22; Jacob’s well in John 4). Wells are life-sources in the desert; their ownership is worth contesting. Christ at Jacob’s well (John 4:7–14) transforms the territorial water-dispute into an offer of living water that transcends all territorial claims: “the water I give them will become in them a spring of water welling up to eternal life” — water no one can seize or dispute.
26: Abimelech said, “I don't know who did this” — the covenant partner’s denial of knowledge of the injustice. The inability of covenant partners to guarantee justice even when they intend it is a recurring limitation of human covenants. Human covenants fail because humans are limited in knowledge and power. Christ as the covenant mediator of the new covenant (Hebrews 9:15) is not limited in this way: “Nothing in all creation is hidden from God’s sight” (Hebrews 4:13). The new covenant’s security rests on the mediator who knows all things and whose power to enforce and fulfill is unlimited.
27: Abraham brought sheep and cattle and gave them to Abimelech, and the two men made a treaty — the covenant ratified by gift and by mutual recognition. The covenant gifts of animals prefigure the sacrificial system that ratifies Israel’s covenant at Sinai (Exodus 24:5–8). The new covenant is ratified by the ultimate gift — not sheep and cattle but the blood of Christ (Hebrews 9:12: “he entered the Most Holy Place once for all by his own blood, thus obtaining eternal redemption”; Hebrews 10:4: “it is impossible for the blood of bulls and goats to take away sins”). The animal covenant-gifts of the patriarchal era are the shadow; Christ’s blood is the substance.
28: Abraham set apart seven ewe lambs from the flock — the seven lambs set apart for the covenant witness at Beer-sheba. Seven is the covenant-completion number in OT ritual (seven days, seven times, seven animals). The setting apart of seven lambs to testify to the well’s ownership is a type of the covenant sign and seal; the Lord’s Supper is the new covenant’s sign, set apart as testimony to Christ’s once-for-all sacrifice (1 Corinthians 11:26: “whenever you eat this bread and drink this cup, you proclaim the Lord’s death until he comes”). The seven lambs are separated from the flock; Christ is the one lamb set apart from eternity (1 Peter 1:20: “chosen before the creation of the world”).
29: Abimelech asked, “What is the meaning of these seven ewe lambs you have set apart by themselves?” — the question about the meaning of the covenant sign. The pattern of covenant sign plus question-and-answer runs through the OT ritual system: Passover (Exodus 12:26–27: “when your children ask… tell them”), the stones at the Jordan (Joshua 4:6–7), the Lord’s Supper (1 Corinthians 11:26: “you proclaim the Lord’s death until he comes”). Every covenant sign is established to prompt inquiry and instruction. The seven lambs here are explained (v30); the Lord’s Supper is explained by Christ himself (Matthew 26:26–28: “This is my body… This is my blood of the covenant”).
30: Abraham replied, “Accept these seven lambs from my hand as a witness that I dug this well” — the seven lambs as legal testimony establishing ownership. The witness-function of the covenant sign — providing evidence for a claim that cannot otherwise be proven — is the function that the Holy Spirit fulfills in the new covenant: “The Spirit himself testifies with our spirit that we are God’s children” (Romans 8:16). The Spirit is the covenant witness who establishes the believer’s inheritance in Christ: “you were marked in him with a seal, the promised Holy Spirit, who is a deposit guaranteeing our inheritance” (Ephesians 1:13–14). The seven lambs as legal witness are the OT type of the Spirit as covenant witness.
31: That place was called Beersheba because the two men swore an oath there — Beer-sheba, “well of the oath” or “well of seven.” The naming of the place commemorates the covenant event. Place-naming as covenant commemoration runs through Genesis (Bethel, Peniel, Beer Lahai Roi) and reaches its fulfillment in the new name given to believers: “I will write on them the name of my God and the name of the city of my God, the new Jerusalem… and I will also write on them my new name” (Revelation 3:12). The name that commemorates the covenant encounter is the type of the new name in Christ that marks permanent belonging.
32: After the covenant was made at Beersheba, Abimelech and Phicol returned to the land of the Philistines — the covenant concluded, the parties return to their territories. The covenant of peace between Abraham and Abimelech is a type of the peace that Christ establishes: “For he himself is our peace, who has made the two groups one and has destroyed the barrier, the dividing wall of hostility” (Ephesians 2:14). The covenants between Abraham and his Gentile neighbors anticipate the larger reconciliation of Jew and Gentile that Christ accomplishes in his body.
33: Abraham planted a tamarisk tree in Beersheba, and there he called on the name of the LORD, the Eternal God (El Olam) — the tamarisk tree as a living covenant monument, and the naming of God as “the Eternal God.” El Olam (the Everlasting God) is used here for the first time. Christ is the embodiment of El Olam’s eternality: “Jesus Christ is the same yesterday and today and forever” (Hebrews 13:8); “In the beginning was the Word, and the Word was with God, and the Word was God” (John 1:1). The tree planted at the covenant site is the living symbol of covenantal permanence; the cross, planted in the ground of Golgotha, is the permanent covenant monument of the new creation.
34: Abraham stayed in the land of the Philistines for a long time — the patience of living peaceably in territory that is not yet fully the covenant possession. The pilgrim existence of Abraham in the Philistines’ land — a sojourner among peoples who are not his covenant community — is the posture that Hebrews 11:13 holds up as the model of faith: “they admitted that they were foreigners and strangers on earth.” Christ’s disciples live the same pilgrim existence: “our citizenship is in heaven” (Philippians 3:20); “you are foreigners and exiles” (1 Peter 2:11). The long sojourn in the Philistines’ land is the type of the church’s long sojourn in the world while awaiting the full inheritance.
